data.title: Tech Heavyweights To Report Next Week, Fed Rate Decision Also In Focus
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street's major averages were mixed on Friday as investors digested the latest developments in the Middle East, including a three-week extension to the truce between Israel and Lebanon. U.S. President Donald Trump announced that the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended for another three weeks after hosting the ambassadors of both countries in Washington. On the economy, the Consumer Sentiment Index was revised up in April-to 49.8 from the initial 47.6 and 53.3 in March-according to the University of Michigan Survey of Consumers.

Investors will be looking forward to the Federal Reserve's interest rate decision next week, which will be out on Wednesday. The consumer confidence data, along with housing numbers, is scheduled for Tuesday. On Thursday, jobs data and the quarterly GDP numbers will be released. S&P Global and ISM PMI data are scheduled for Friday.

_______________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday: Verizon (VZ). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday: Visa (V), Coca-Cola (KO), Starbucks (SBUX), British American Tobacco (BTI). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday: Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), AbbVie (ABBV), Qualcomm (QCOM), Ford (F). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday: Apple (AAPL), Eli Lilly (LLY), Merck (MRK), Mastercard (MA). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday: Exxon Mobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Car rental stocks Avis Budget (CAR) and Hertz Global (HTZ) are set up for another volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Pitney Bowes (PBI), Avanos Medical (AVNS), and Skillz (SKLZ). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Northrop Grumman (NOC), Lockheed (LMT), and Tractor Supply (TSCO). Short interest is elevated on SoundHound AI (SOUN) and BigBear.ai Holdings (BBAI) again.

IPO watch: Pershing Square USA (PSUS) is expected to price its IPO and begin trading. Buyers of the new company will get exposure to Bill Ackman's investing vehicle, plus a small stake in the Pershing Square (PS) management company. The analyst's quiet period expires on HMH Holding (HMH) to free up analysts to post ratings.

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Cal-Maine (CALM), AGNC Invest (AGNC), Morgan Stanley (MS), and Costco (COST).

Apple Earnings Preview: Apple is expected to report second-quarter earnings of $1.95 per share on revenue of $109.69B next week. Analysts expect the company to maintain its momentum driven by robust iPhone demand in China and India, despite supply chain constraints and chip cost pressures. In Q2 2025, the gross margin is estimated to reach between 48-49%, up from 48.16% in Q1 2026. The earnings commentary is likely to revolve around the company's most recent announcement, where Apple named John Ternus as Tim Cook's successor. Investors are likely to be keenly looking out for the management's further comments on it. On the AI front, analysts at BofA Global Research see Apple's M5 family as a meaningful step towards the company's AI compute stack, given that M5 is AI-optimized to run workloads like diffusion models on devices. "AAPL looks relatively insulated from the AI-related volatility," they added.

Alphabet Earnings Preview: Alphabet is projected to post Q1 EPS of $2.63 and revenue of $106.93B. Ahead of the earnings, analysts expect a short-term volatility due to elevated CapEx, market expectations, and potential pressure on free cash flow. Alphabet Inc. has already outlined its substantial capital expenditure plans for the year, and the first quarter should offer an early glimpse into that investment ramp-up. They have also expressed concerns over the rising popularity of Gemini potentially cannibalising its major revenue generator, Google Search. Despite the concerns, a solid Q1 print is expected, especially on the back of Google Cloud. "Even if CapEx is a delicate issue, if this (Google Cloud) business division maintains strong top-line growth, with healthy margins and a strong backlog, it will be an excellent signal that the business is sustainable and that demand is real and consistent," according to a Seeking Alpha analyst.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Up for a challenge? Test your knowledge on the biggest events in the investing world over the past week. Take the latest Seeking Alpha News Quiz and see how you stack up against the competition.


Wall Street’s major averages ended the week mixed, even as the S&P 500 (SP500) and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) jumped to new highs as the conflict in the Middle East continued to lack resolution while oil prices rose.

Earlier this week, U.S. President Donald Trump said the U.S. would extend its ceasefire with Iran until a coordinated plan for an end to the war, which would include Israel, was brought forward. However, he has left the ongoing blockade in place.

He also accused Iran of violating a ceasefire agreement after Iranian forces fired upon ships in the Strait of Hormuz, including a French vessel and a British freighter. Later, Trump ordered the U.S. Navy to “shoot and kill” any vessel found placing mines in the Strait of Hormuz with “no hesitation."

Iran has said it will not participate in a second round of talks with the U.S., objecting to what it described as excessive and unrealistic demands from Washington. Also, Iran’s parliament speaker, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, has reportedly stepped down as Tehran’s lead negotiator.

Lastly, Trump announced that the ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon will be extended for another three weeks after hosting the ambassadors of both countries in Washington.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) increased around 13.2% this week to settle around $94, and Brent futures (CO1:COM) also rose 17.2% to $105 per barrel as of post-market Friday.

In economic news, retail sales jumped more than expected in March, while February business inventories rose +0.4% MoM to $2,686.8B vs. +0.2% consensus, according to the U.S. Census Bureau.

Also, the AAII survey showed that bullish sentiment among individual investors surged, while bearish views declined. Lastly, the Consumer Sentiment Index was revised up in April to 49.8 from the initial 47.6 and 53.3 in March.

For the week, the S&P (SP500) gained +0.6%, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) rose +1.5% and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) declined -0.4%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.


Importers vs. consumers

Customs and Border Protection launched a tariff refund portal this week, with thousands of companies rushing to file claims for the duties they paid over the past year. It's unclear how many of them will pass the benefit on to consumers, but retailers like Walmart (WMT) and Target (TGT) stand to receive billions in refunds, while the tech, media and telecom industry could get over $47B. Read more.

Seeking Alpha's Calls Of The Week

Berkshire (BRK.B): Abel Arbitrage, Strong Returns Post-Buffett.

Star Bulk's (SBLK) Updated Dividend Policy, Macro Tailwinds.

Trump's Green Light - Cannabis Stocks (MSOS) Are Surging.

The Worst Is Now Over, Here's Why It's Time To Buy FSCO.

Orion Properties (ONL): Refinancing Risk Is Off The Table.

Enterprise Products (EPD) May See Limited Upside In 2026.

Enbridge (ENB): Moving To The Sidelines After A Great Run.

Locking In Gains - Sell Amazon (AMZN) On Anthropic Spike.

Campbell's (CPB) Is A Sell Amid Troubling Core Inefficiencies.

Here's Why It's Time To Move On From Coterra Energy (CTRA).

Weekly Movement

U.S. Indices
Dow -0.4% to 49,231. S&P 500 +0.6% to 7,165. Nasdaq +1.5% to 24,837. Russell 2000 +0.4% to 2,787. CBOE Volatility Index +7% to 18.71.

S&P 500 Sectors
Consumer Staples +1.2%. Utilities +0.1%. Financials -1.9%. Telecom -0.8%. Healthcare -3.1%. Industrials -0.6%. Information Technology +3.1%. Materials +0.1%. Energy +3.2%. Consumer Discretionary -0.3%. Real Estate -1.4%.

World Indices
London -2.7% to 10,379. France -3.2% to 8,158. Germany -2.3% to 24,129. Japan +2.1% to 59,716. China +0.7% to 4,080. Hong Kong -0.7% to 25,978. India -2.3% to 76,681.

Commodities and Bonds
Crude Oil WTI +12.6% to $94.4/bbl. Gold -2.8% to $4,740.9/oz. Natural Gas -5.7% to 2.523. Ten-Year Bond Yield -0.2 bps to 4.31.

Forex and Cryptos
EUR/USD -0.36%. USD/JPY +0.47%. GBP/USD +0.12%. Bitcoin +2.6%. Litecoin +1.8%. Ethereum -1.4%. XRP +0.3%.

Top S&P 500 Gainers
Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) +25%. United Rentals (URI) +22%. Texas Instruments (TXN) +21%. Intel (INTC) +21%. ON Semiconductor (ON) +19%.

Top S&P 500 Losers
Charter Communications (CHTR) -24%. Tractor Supply (TSCO) -18%. lululemon athletica (LULU) -14%. Northrop Grumman (NOC) -14%. Lockheed Martin (LMT) -13%.

Where will the markets be headed next week? Current trends and ideas? Add your thoughts to the comments section.

data.title: First Industrial Realty: A Stress-Free REIT With Reliable Growth Drivers
data.description:
Industrial real estate has gone through a reset over the past two years. Demand cooled from pandemic highs, supply briefly surged, and investors started asking harder questions about durability. Think of REITs like Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR), whose valuation has fallen on hard times as a result.

But beneath that noise, a handful of operators have kept executing with discipline. First Industrial Realty Trust (FR) is one of them, and its latest results suggest the story is shifting back toward steady growth paired with reliable income.

I last covered FR back in February 2025, highlighting its solid operating fundamentals. Since then, FR has delivered a respectable 15.4% total return. In this article, I revisit the stock and discuss why it’s a ‘Buy’ on strong execution, so let’s get started!

A Focused Industrial Platform With Embedded Growth

First Industrial Realty Trust is a pure-play industrial REIT, owning and developing logistics properties across key U.S. markets. Its portfolio is built around distribution hubs, infill locations, and development-ready land. These are assets that benefit from long-term trends like e-commerce, supply chain modernization, and domestic manufacturing.

As shown below, its markets are strategically centered around supply-constrained Tier 1 and growing Sunbelt markets.

FR’s strategy has been consistent for years. It prioritizes spec development in high-demand markets, maintains a flexible land bank, and rotates capital when values are compelling. That approach is now paying off in multiple ways.

One of the most notable recent developments is a pending $131 million land sale in Phoenix’s 303 corridor. The pricing, about $30 per land square foot, represents more than three times prevailing industrial land values in that market.

This is not just a one-off gain. It highlights the embedded value within the company’s land portfolio and its ability to monetize assets opportunistically.

Earnings Reflect Strength Beneath the Surface

First Industrial’s first-quarter results were steady on the surface but stronger when you look underneath.

Funds from operations came in at $0.68 per share, flat year over year. Adjusted for one-time advisory costs tied to a proxy contest, FFO would have been $0.72 per share. More importantly, operating fundamentals showed clear momentum.

Cash same-store NOI grew 8.7%, driven by higher rental rates, reduced concessions, and contractual rent bumps. Leasing spreads remain robust, with overall cash rental rate increases hitting 41% on new and renewal leases. That figure was boosted by a major Southern California renewal. But even excluding that, rent growth remains well above historical norms.

Occupancy held firm at 94.3%, and FR has already addressed 61% of its 2026 lease expirations. That early progress reduces rollover risk and locks in higher rents ahead of time.

Industry Tailwinds Are Stabilizing And Turning Positive

The broader industrial backdrop is beginning to fall in favor again. National vacancy held steady at 6.7% in the first quarter. Net absorption reached 43 million square feet, only modestly trailing new supply. Meanwhile, construction starts remain muted, and the development pipeline is highly pre-leased at 39%.

The combination of steady demand, slowing supply, and disciplined development sets the stage for tightening fundamentals over time. FR is already seeing early signs of this shift. Touring activity by prospective tenants is increasing, and decision-making is accelerating, particularly for spaces under 200,000 square feet.

Importantly, management does not see geopolitical risks, including Middle East tensions, as having a measurable impact on leasing so far. That stability reinforces the resilience of the underlying demand drivers.

Full-year FFO guidance remains in the range of $3.09 to $3.19 per share, excluding one-time costs. Same-store NOI growth is expected at 5% to 6%, with occupancy projected between 94% and 95%.

There is also a subtle but important shift underway. As supply growth slows and demand stabilizes, pricing power is holding. Rent growth in Southern California has flattened sequentially but remains positive, and long-term trends still support further increases.

Financial Strength and Disciplined Capital Allocation

FR maintains a solid BBB-rated balance sheet with a safe net debt-to-TTM EBITDA ratio of 5.0x. This supports the primary growth engine of speculative development, which typically generates higher returns than acquisitions. Historically, about 20% to 25% of capital has gone toward acquiring stabilized properties, with the majority directed toward development.

At the same time, FR is increasingly willing to recycle capital. The Phoenix land sale is a prime example, in which it monetized a non-income-producing asset at an attractive valuation and redeployed proceeds elsewhere.

FR currently yields a respectable 3.3%, and the dividend is well covered by a 64% payout ratio. It also comes with a 5-year CAGR of 12.5% and 12 consecutive years of growth.

FR doesn’t appear to be cheap on the surface at the current price of $61.37 with a forward P/FFO of 19.5. However, I believe the valuation is well-justified considering FR’s strong cash leasing trend and forward outlook. I believe FR is positioned to grow FFO per share in the high single-digit range over the medium term. This, combined with the dividend yield, could lead to total returns in the low teens annually.

Risks to Watch

Leasing velocity for large spaces continues to lag smaller footprints. Decision-making could remain slow in that segment, delaying occupancy gains. Supply, while moderating, is still working through the system in certain markets. Areas like the Inland Empire have elevated availability in mid-sized spaces, which could pressure rents in the near term.

There are also execution risks tied to development. Timing, lease-up pace, and construction costs all matter, particularly for speculative projects. Plus, while data center-related demand is growing, it is not yet a major driver. If broader industrial demand weakens, that segment alone would not be enough to offset it.

Investor Takeaway

First Industrial Realty is not a flashy story. It is a steady one, and that is exactly the appeal. It’s combining strong leasing economics, disciplined development, and opportunistic capital recycling to drive both income and growth. Rent spreads remain elevated, occupancy is stable, and embedded land value provides additional upside.

At the same time, industry conditions are shifting back in its favor. Supply is moderating, and demand is broad-based, supporting FR’s pricing power. For investors, this creates a compelling long-term setup.

Investors are getting a REIT with reliable cash flow and visible rent growth, with multiple levers for value creation. In the current market that often swings between extremes, that kind of consistency is worth paying attention to for sleep-well-at-night returns. For these reasons, I maintain a ‘Buy’ rating on FR.

data.title: Selling The Furniture To Pay The Rent: The Unsustainable Reality Of Strategy, Inc. Preferred
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

We don't usually write bearish articles. We find that it is usually much more constructive to talk about the things we want to buy as opposed to talking about the much larger number of things that we choose not to buy.

However, there is a group of investments that members have been frequently asking about, and it is an investment vehicle that is as terrible an idea as "CDO squared" back in 2008. Unfortunately, it is created specifically to appeal to the greed of income investors.

We are talking about several issues of preferred securities issued by Strategy Inc. (MSTR). We love preferred equities, and we own a lot of them at High Dividend Opportunities. We have a preference for preferred equities that have a higher than average yield and are with companies outside of the traditional banks and insurance companies that dominate the preferred asset class. So naturally, some members are looking at the high yields of the MSTR preferred shares and think they look great.

We don't own any, we would not add them to the model portfolio, and we strongly urge anyone considering buying these to completely understand the risk they are taking.

What Is MSTR?

MSTR is a software company that has a modest and slightly profitable software business. They started buying Bitcoin (BTC), and today, their Bitcoin holdings dwarf their software business. MSTR now describes itself as a Bitcoin Treasury Company. Which in Plain English means "We own a bunch of Bitcoin so you should buy our stock". Because that's it, they own and hold Bitcoin and have a little software side business.

MSTR owns a lot of Bitcoin. As of December 31st 2025, they owned $58.8 billion in Bitcoin. Source

Plus about $2.3 billion in cash, which makes up substantially all of their assets. What does MSTR do with this Bitcoin? Nothing. They just hold it and buy more.

MSTR's revenues come from product licenses, subscription services, and product support – totaling about $477 million last year. Their gross profit in 2025 was just $327.8 million, or about 0.5% return on their assets.

That's before they pay for marketing, R&D, and G&A. In other words, against the backdrop of their Bitcoin portfolio which declined in value by $5.4 billion last year, their revenues are quite insignificant.

What MSTR Is Doing Today

MSTR seems to have little interest in the software portion of the company. Instead, they are focused on buying as much Bitcoin as they can. With minimal organic cash flow from the company, MSTR is buying Bitcoin by raising equity. It has been issuing common equity and now preferred equity in massive amounts.

Most recently, MSTR issued $1.0028 billion of the preferred STRC, which was used to acquire $1 billion worth of Bitcoin. In essence, MSTR funded a purchase of Bitcoin purely with capital raised from the preferred.

MSTR has authorization to issue massive amounts of equity, primarily through $21.6 billion of STRC and $27.1 billion in common equity.

Here is our estimate of MSTR's outstanding preferred after this issuance based on their dashboard at Strategy.com:

They have 104 million preferred shares outstanding, and they are paying cash dividends of $277.97 million per quarter, or about $1.11 billion/year.

Remember, their annual gross revenue had ranged from $463 million to $496 million over the past three years. MSTR does not have the cash flow to even pay for one quarter of dividends from its operating business.

How Is MSTR Paying The Preferred?

With no recurring cash flow, MSTR can only pay for the preferred dividends in one of three ways:

  • Utilizing cash on hand. MSTR has approximately $2.25 billion in cash according to their dashboard at Strategy.com. That's enough to pay dividends on the current preferred for about two years.
  • Issuing more equity. MSTR can issue either common or preferred equity to raise cash. This can help with immediate liquidity, but in the case of preferred issuance, it leads to higher dividend obligations in the future, and it dilutes the common if they go that route.

  • Selling Bitcoin. MSTR has $60.4 billion in bitcoin at current prices. Their average cost of Bitcoin is $59 billion, so they have a small gain at today's prices. The problem with that is that MSTR then has less Bitcoin and would need the price to go up faster to continue paying the preferred. This works great if Bitcoin only goes up in price.

So, MSTR is issuing preferred equity like it is going out of style, and its plan is to either keep issuing equity or to sell its assets to pay the dividends. This would be a wonderful plan if you knew that Bitcoin is going to be $100,000 next year.

If Bitcoin is say $50,000, then to pay the $1.1 billion in dividends for the year, MSTR would need to sell 22k Bitcoin. It then has a smaller base from which to pay the $1.1 billion the following year. Every sale, MSTR realizes permanent capital losses, and the amount that needs to be sold to honor the preferred dividends increases. A year or two is probably manageable. If Bitcoin goes down in price and stays down, it's unsustainable.

In other words, preferred shareholders need Bitcoin to go up in price in order for their dividends to be sustained. As MSTR issues more preferred equity, the higher the risk to the previously existing preferred. MSTR is showing no indication of slowing down; it is prudent to assume that it's going to continue issuing equity and buying more Bitcoin. And the amount owed for the annual dividend will increase.

STRC

It's worth noting that the preferred that MSTR is issuing the most of is STRC, which has a variable dividend. STRC's dividend is adjusted in an attempt to keep the price trading at $100. So if the price drops, the dividend will be raised so that investors buy more of it.

That sounds great, right? If it becomes less popular, the price drops, and you'll get a dividend raise!

It is important to note that this is not guaranteed. From the prospectus:

"Our current intention, which is subject to change in our sole and absolute discretion, is to adjust the regular dividend rate in such a manner as we believe will maintain STRC Stock’s trading price at or close to its stated amount of $100 per share."

In other words, it is their "intention", and they can change it whenever they want, and there isn't a thing you can do about it. It's a gimmick, and it's designed to help MSTR continue to issue as much STRC as possible near $100/share. It reminds us of the "non-traded" preferred issued by a REIT, Ashford Hospitality Trust (AHT), which had provisions that allowed for preferred shareholders to demand redemption for cash or common equity at par value after a period of time. This incentivized shareholders to buy because they thought they could wait out the waiting period and then just demand redemption at par and be "guaranteed" to get their money back.

Well, they ended up with too many preferred outstanding, and decided to suspend redemptions and the dividends.

We aren't predicting that the same will happen with MSTR, but it certainly is something that can happen. MSTR can stop increasing the dividend and let the price of STRC crash whenever it wants to, and it can suspend the dividends.

For now, MSTR wants to issue more shares, so keeping the price elevated is worth the extra costs. The day MSTR decides it doesn't need or want to issue more shares, the variable dividend is going to get reduced. If Bitcoin prices are low, it could be suspended completely.

Conclusion

The bottom line is that MSTR's preferred shares rely on Bitcoin going up in price. It might. Maybe Bitcoin is headed to $1 million. I've seen people make that claim. Our point is that for these investments, it is an affirmative bet that Bitcoin is going to go up in price at a fairly steady pace. If it doesn't, then the preferreds are not going to collect all their dividends, and they aren't going to recover par. MSTR is placing a big bet on Bitcoin using other people's money, and if you are buying the preferred, it's your money that is being bet with. You're taking the risk on Bitcoin, but if it does go up, you're only getting a small piece of the reward. If it goes down, you're losing your money as assuredly as if you just bought Bitcoin yourself.

If you are going to take on the risk of Bitcoin prices, you should make sure you are also getting the potential reward if Bitcoin continues to go up and outperform. These preferred take on the risk, but pass along a large portion of the reward to the common shareholders.

Invest in preferred stock with companies that have recurring cash flow to cover their distributions. If you want to take a portion of those dividends and put it in Bitcoin, great.

This type of financial engineering doesn't end well in my experience. Buying a preferred equity that is entirely dependent upon issuing new equity or liquidating the asset base isn't a good idea. CDO Squared worked great as long as housing prices only went up. MSTR preferred work great as long as Bitcoin only goes up.

data.title: MakeMyTrip: Rising Competition, Demanding Valuation
data.description:
MakeMyTrip’s (MMYT) share price has dropped by over 50% over the past year due to a number of negative factors, including a short seller report alleging anti-competitive practices as well as macro headwinds such as the rupee depreciation. This substantial share price drop prompted a closer look; however, despite a 50% valuation reset, MakeMyTrip is a cautious Hold or even a Sell, as it is still 'priced to perfection' against an increasingly competitive operating environment.

Financial performance

After a solid couple of years, MakeMyTrip's latest financial performance has been normalizing; revenues over the past three quarters (ended December 2025) increased around 8% YoY (12% in constant currency), dropping from the 26% YoY reported revenue growth notched in the same period the previous financial year, which benefited from strong industry post-covid tailwinds. All segments grew on a constant currency basis; Air Ticketing revenues rose 4.4% YoY, which was in line with official 4%-6% domestic travel projections for the period; hotels & Packages segment revenues rose 7% YoY, keeping up with industry projections of 7%-8% in 2025; bus ticketing segment revenues jumped 31% YoY, while their Others segment surged 36% YoY, suggesting MakeMyTrip is successfully emerging as a travel superapp.

Net profit, however, sank more than 50% to $27 million for the nine-month period, largely on the back of non-cash finance costs, which more than tripled owing to their issuance of zero-coupon convertible debt to buy back Trip.com’s stake.

Domestic travel opportunity is a key growth driver

Looking ahead, I view domestic travel as a likely growth driver for MakeMyTrip, which has a heavy domestic presence (offices are mostly located in India) and the largest market share (over 50% domestic OTA market share). For perspective, around 6.5 billion domestic trips were made in China, while in India, it was just over 1.5 billion, indicating a market with the potential to expand fourfold. Domestic travel growth could be supported by continued economic growth; India's GDP growth is projected at 6.4% this year, and medium-term annual growth is projected at above 6% through 2030.

Competitive pressures could dampen company growth prospects

While domestic travel presents a long growth runway for the industry as a whole, rising competitive pressures could dampen MMYT's growth prospects. Accommodation platforms Booking Holdings and Airbnb have growth ambitions in India, and their global scale, and strongholds on higher value foreign travelers present competitive advantages they can leverage to opportunistically nibble away at MMYT's domestic market share on the back of rising inbound tourism to India.

International tourist arrivals to India are over 20 million, compared with over 30 million to China and over 70 million to the U.S. Airbnb (ABNB) and Booking (BKNG) are more likely than not already serving as primary OTA platforms to this customer base, and they likely will make efforts to increase their Indian footprint as well to cater to increasing demand. Switching costs in the form of UX familiarity and payment frictions will likely keep these international travelers tethered to established global OTAs, making them their preferred choice for bookings even in India, unless MakeMyTrip offers a vastly superior offering and price, which at this stage is not a likely scenario.

At this stage I don’t see MakeMyTrip competing head-on against international incumbents outside India; Booking.com has over 28 million listings worldwide, Airbnb 9 million listings worldwide, versus MMYT’s 1.2 million properties worldwide (source: Annual Report 2024). Therefore, more likely than not, while outbound travel has so far been a major growth driver for MMYT's Air Ticketing segment, helping offset weaker domestic travel business over the past few quarters, international competitors including Booking.com and Airbnb are more likely better positioned to capture a significant proportion of these outbound travelers' accommodation spend. The growth opportunity is not insignificant; just around 7% of Indians hold passports (with over 92 million passport holders compared with 160 million in China), suggesting a market still at early stages offering a potentially two-fold growth opportunity as the market matures, giving ample time for international rivals to expand their footprint and chip away at MMYT’s market-leading domestic share in the process. Airbnb India reported a 50% increase in bookings during the December 2025 quarter, with first-time bookings up by 60% (in comparison, MakeMyTrip’s Hotels & Packages gross bookings rose 10% YoY during the same quarter), reflecting solid traction.

OTA disintermediation by vendor partners is a major risk

MMYT's top five hotel partners by gross bookings include major hotel chains such as Accor Hotels and Marriott. These chains, Marriott (MAR) in particular (which has reported that as much as 38% of bookings are direct bookings), are increasingly sidelining middlemen such as MakeMyTrip, in an effort to improve margins and better serve customers through direct relationships. From the customer’s point of view, perks such as free room nights, free air miles, upgrades, as well as better customer service, particularly with regard to cancellations, and refunds, present compelling reasons to make direct bookings with vendors. A similar dynamic is playing out among airlines as well.

MMYT’s customer inducement costs amounted to 25% in 9M FY 2026 (three quarters ended December 2025), up from 23% of revenues in 9M FY 2025 (three quarters ended December 2024). This is not a particularly encouraging sign, and if industry trends continue moving towards direct bookings, particularly among the biggest vendors, the impact on revenues and earnings could be significant for MMYT.

Valuation

MakeMyTrip’s free cash flows are roughly around $120 million or so (not adding back stock-based compensation of around $36 million and not adjusting for over $60 million of non-cash finance costs), as these represent economic costs to equity holders. With their market cap of around $4.6 billion, this implies an FCF yield of nearly 2%, which is far lower than their cost of equity of around 10% (risk free rate = 4.3%, Beta = 0.83, equity risk premium estimated at 7%). The company would need to quintuple their free cash flows to justify their valuation. A DCF model would indicate this is achievable with consistent 25% revenue growth over a five-year period (which would triple revenues to around $3 billion) while increasing FCF margins from around 12% currently to around 20% (at which point their net margins would be comparable to global peers including Booking.com). In my view this is a tall order, for reasons including intensifying competition potentially eroding their dominant position, vendor disintermediation, and the resulting cap to growth and margins. While it could be argued that Bus Ticketing and their Other segment are showing positive growth and have little to no competition, it remains to be seen whether they can sufficiently offset earnings and revenue impact to businesses like the hotels, packages, and flights, which are likely higher margin than bus ticketing.

It could be argued that non-cash finance costs should be added to their free cash flows, as these are implied costs with no immediate cash outflow. However, I take a more conservative view, as the Trip.com convertible debt transaction valued MMYT at around $6 billion, which, in my view, may have been an overvaluation, which means there is a real possibility this would remain as an outstanding debt and therefore a cost that needs to be accounted for.

Risks

Major international OTAs, including Airbnb and Booking.com, have operated for over a decade in India and so far have had limited success in dislodging MakeMyTrip, which still retains a market-leading position and still remains the more popular travel app by downloads (MMYT ranked 8th versus Airbnb ranked 10th among Android downloads, and 7th versus 13th among iPhone downloads), though to be fair, MakeMyTrip had a 15-year head start over Airbnb. If MakeMyTrip manages to retain or even solidify their lead and bargaining power while delivering better than anticipated margin expansion, for instance, through customer loyalty (thereby lowering customer inducement expenses) or cultural missteps on the part of international OTAs, AI-driven cost improvements, their valuation could be justified.

Takeaway

After a more than 50% correction, MMYT’s share price now sports a Strong Buy analyst consensus rating and Strong Sell Quant rating. In my view, it still appears to be priced to perfection, suggesting limited margin for error despite the recent correction; while they stand to benefit from industry growth, extremely stiff competition presents significant hurdles to MMYT’s growth and valuation. Hesitations over competitive pressures and a valuation that looks priced to perfection make me view this as a cautious hold or sell.

data.title: CSHI: Solid T-Bills And Options ETF, Positive Spread To T-Bills, Solid Track-Record
data.description:
Although rates on t-bills and assorted cash securities are down these past few years, these remain at reasonably good levels, and much higher than they were during the 2010s. Cash ETFs remain solid investments, and the Neos Enhanced Income Cash Alternative ETF (CSHI) is a particularly unique, strong cash ETF.

CSHI focuses on t-bills with smaller equity option spread trades. The fund's strategy boosts dividend yields by around 0.50% - 0.90% above those of t-bills, so expect dividend yields to settle in the 4.1% - 4.5% in the coming months. Higher income should, and has, boosted long-term returns. Risks are a bit higher, and the fund should see small, single-digit losses during severe bear markets, marginal, temporary losses during most such scenarios. In my opinion, the increased income more than outweighs the increased risk, making CSHI a strong cash ETF, and a buy.

CSHI - Strategy, Portfolio and Investment Thesis

CSHI focuses on t-bills, with these accounting for around 99.5% of its portfolio, and most of its income.

CSHI also invests or trades in S&P 500 put option spreads. In simple terms, these operate as selling equity market insurance: CSHI gets premiums from their counterparties in exchange for paying up if the S&P 500 collapses suddenly. CSHI's options are structured in such a way that risks are minimal, single-digit drawdowns from sudden double-digit equity market losses, income low, in the 0.5% - 1.0% range. Selling insurance is generally profitable, even after accounting for any payouts, as should, and has, been the case for CSHI in the past.

Let's look into one of CSHI's option spreads, to see how these work in a bit more detail. Take special note of the spread in yellow.

In the example above, CSHI bought and sold put options on the S&P 500.

Selling the put option generates some money in premiums for the fund, boosting its dividend yield.

Selling the put option means losses if the S&P 500 declines below its 6,375 strike price by its 04/27/2026 expiration date, next week. The S&P 500 trades at a 7,165 price right now, so the index would have to decline by around 11% before CSHI starts seeing any losses from this trade. Although the S&P 500 does suffer double-sigit drawdowns with some regularity, these rarely occur in the space of a week. Losses are obviously possible, but unlikely, even during recessions or bear markets.

To further limit risks, the fund bought another put option, this one with a 6,100 strike price, same expiration date. Said option means profits for the fund if the S&P 500 were to decline below 6,100, capping losses at said level. On the flipside, buying said option is costly, reducing the fund's income.

Doing further calculations considering all the option spreads here and their characteristics, losses are capped at around 4%, which should occur from sudden, double-digit S&P 500 losses. Premiums can't be estimated from the data provided by the fund, but CSHI generally yields around 0.50% - 0.90% more than benchmark t-bills ETFs. Accounting for expenses, 0.39% for CSHI versus 0.09% for the largest t-bill ETF in the market, these spreads seem to generate around 0.80% - 1.20% in premiums. Right now, at least.

CSHI's option trades always generate a positive amount of premiums, due to their structure / the strike prices and amounts selected. Magnitudes vary, depending on the specifics of the options, as well as option prices (in turn dependent on market volatility). In my opinion, and based on the fund's track-record, the premiums should more than cover the fund's excess fees moving forward. Spreads might still decline, as was the case during 2024 (see above). Overall, spreads are quite wide right now, so the fund's prospective returns look reasonably good.

Summarizing, CSHI's option trades result in around 0.50% - 0.9% in extra income, net of fees. On the flipside, the fund could see around 4% in price losses from sudden, double-digit S&P 500 drawdowns. In my opinion, this is a good tradeoff, making the fund a solid investment opportunity. More risk-averse investors might disagree and consider the fact that these specific figures are likely to fluctuate moving forward. Spreads were tighter in the past.

CSHI - Other Considerations

In my opinion, CSHI's strategy and its major implications are the most important factors for investors to consider. Still, will go through some of the more traditional quantitative metrics for the fund, see how it stacks up to peers.

CSHI's 4.8% dividend yield is quite good, higher than that of most bonds.

Drawdowns and realized volatility are both extremely low, albeit materially higher than t-bills. Still closer to cash than bonds, however.

Although the fund has yet to experience a prolonged recession, it did experience a sharp bear market in early 2025, from the imposition of tariffs and the resultant market turmoil. Equity losses were sharp, of the sort that could spell trouble for CSHI. Although there were small single-digit losses as equity prices collapsed, these ultimately proved temporary, with the fund recovering in around a month. I do see some marginal price losses at the 6m mark, probably due to volatility, as I'm not really seeing any impact on total returns.

CSHI's performance track-record is reasonably good, with the fund outperforming t-bills most bonds since inception. Outperformance was due to its good dividends, and slightly favorable macro conditions, with the fund benefitting from elevated Fed rates throughout its existence, especially in prior years.

Of special note is CSHI outperforming the State Street SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (BIL), one of the largest t-bill index ETFs in the market, for 2.75% these past three years, roughly equivalent to a 0.91% CAGR.

CSHI has outperformed t-bills since I last covered the fund, in mid-2025, but has underperformed relative to most other types of bonds. In my opinion, the fund is likely to see broadly similar long-term performance moving forward: its spreads generate sufficient excess income to outperform t-bills, less so most other types of bonds. Much will depend on Federal Reserve policy and broader market movements, however.

Conclusion

CSHI focuses on t-bills with smaller equity option spread trades. CSHI's strategy results in around 0.50% - 0.90% more in income than t-bills, with the possibility of around 4% in losses from significant, sharp S&P 500 drawdowns. In my opinion, the increased income more than outweighs the increased risk, making CSHI a strong cash ETF, and a buy.

data.title: Kinder Morgan: A Top Income Pick For 2026
data.description:
Kinder Morgan (KMI) reported higher-than-expected Q1’26 earnings, driven by double-digit earnings growth in the natural gas business. Kinder Morgan is in an advantageous position as one of North America’s leading energy transporters that connects producer areas such as the Permian to consumer end-markets, and as such is set to benefit from rising natural gas demand. Further, the midstream platform has a $10B investment backlog, which is set to boost the firm's earnings and distributable growth prospects.

In the most recent quarter, Kinder Morgan grew its EBITDA by 18% year-over-year to a record $2.5B, boosted by the pipeline operator’s sizable natural gas operations. I like Kinder Morgan for its large size, potential for earnings-accretive pipeline acquisitions, and dividend growth. Shares also trade below my fair value estimate of $34, meaning investors not only collect a 4% yield, but also have capital appreciation potential.

Previous rating

Kinder Morgan was a strong buy for me back in January 2026 -- Strong Growth, Natural Gas Focus, AI Catalyst -- mainly because of the firm’s exposure to the AI Data Center industry that is hungry for enormous amounts of electricity. With AI adoption accelerating across industries, the need for GPU compute, and therefore energy sources such as natural gas, is a major driver for the operator’s earnings and distributable cash flow growth in the future. With Kinder Morgan having a massive backlog, I believe the midstream enterprise remains a bet on both dividend and earnings growth in 2026.

Kinder Morgan’s natural gas business is on fire

The midstream energy enterprise beat expectations on both the top and the bottom line on April 22, 2026, amid robust demand for natural gas, primarily Kinder Morgan’s largest business segment: KMI published adjusted EPS of $0.48, beating the analyst consensus figure of $0.39 per share easily. KMI's revenues rose ~14% year-over-year to $4.83B, coming in at $281M above the consensus figure.

Kinder Morgan is one of the largest midstream platforms in the North American energy market, as it transports approximately 40% of America's natural gas from producers to end markets. The company owns an interest in or operates ~82,000 miles of pipelines. This includes a natural gas pipeline network that encompasses 65,000 miles and is complemented by more than 700 billion cubic feet (BCF) of working gas storage capacity (~15% of all natural gas storage in the U.S.).

KMI has an operational presence in major production hubs, such as the Permian, Bakken, Utica, and Haynesville Basins (among others), and it is therefore one of the most geographically diversified midstream platforms in the U.S. energy sector.

Kinder Morgan's natural gas business is what drove the firm's outperformance in Q1'26. In the most recent quarter, natural gas pipelines earned Kinder Morgan $1.7B on an EBDA level (essentially EBITDA without the recognition of interest expenses), showing ~18% year-over-year growth. Since natural gas is by far Kinder Morgan's largest business, rising natural gas demand is the single biggest growth catalyst for the pipeline operator. The long-term outlook for U.S. natural gas consumption is positive: according to KMI's internal projections, natural gas demand is set to grow to 150 billion cubic feet per day (Bcf/d) by 2031, highlighting a 27% increase compared to current levels. Since natural gas is the biggest segment for KMI, representing 68% of EBDA, the segment will remain key to Kinder Morgan's growth going forward.

A strong fee structure supports the value proposition for income investors

The most important factor for me to even consider Kinder Morgan as an income investment relates to the midstream platform’s high (96%) percentage of cash flows that are linked to some kind of fee-based contract (i.e., take-or-pay). Take-or-pay contracts, which represent the majority of fee-based contracts (65%) for Kinder Morgan, obligate a customer to pay for a specific capacity or volume even if they don't use the underlying commodity.

Fee-based contracts offer investors the opportunity to take advantage of pre-determined energy prices for the transport of energy raw materials such as natural gas, crude oil, or petrochemicals, which means midstream companies tend to have a lot of cash flow (and dividend) visibility as a result.

Dividend coverage trend

Kinder Morgan has stopped reporting DCF per-share figures in 2024 to align its reporting with standard equity measures in the midstream industry, such as adjusted EPS and free cash flow. However, the midstream enterprise in its supplemental quarterly schedules retroactively discloses its quarterly DCF per-share figure, which is still a useful measure to calculate a midstream platform's dividend coverage ratio, mainly because it strips away non-cash accounting distortions, such as depreciation.

For the most recent quarter, Kinder Morgan did not disclose its quarterly DCF per-share yet, but this figure can be approximated by making certain adjustments to adjusted EBITDA.

I derive Kinder Morgan's per-share DCF by subtracting ~$430M in interest expenses, $220M in maintenance capital (which reconciles to KMI's budget for 2026), and $95M in cash taxes from Kinder Morgan's Q1'26 adjusted EBITDA of $2,539M. Dividing this resulting figure of $1,794M by 2.2B outstanding shares yields an estimated DCF figure of $0.81 per share. This figure in turn implies a dividend coverage ratio of 2.72X for the first fiscal quarter.

I highlight Kinder Morgan's trend in DCF as well as in the coverage ratio in the table below.

KMI Q1'25 Q2'25 Q3'25 Q4'25 Q1'26 (est)
Distributable Cash Flow $0.66 $0.52 $0.56 $0.68 $0.81
Declared Dividends $0.2925 $0.2925 $0.2925 $0.2925 $0.2975
Coverage 2.26 X 1.78 X 1.91 X 2.32 X 2.72 X

Source: Author

For the current fiscal year, Kinder Morgan targets approximately $2.52 per share in distributable cash (according to its latest budget plan), implying 4% year-over-year growth. Since the midstream platform raised its dividend by 2% at the beginning of the year to $0.2975 per share quarterly, Kinder Morgan is on track to publish a forward full-year dividend coverage ratio of 2.12X vs. 2.07X in FY 2025. From a dividend coverage perspective, therefore, Kinder Morgan pays a very well-supported 4% dividend yield that appears to be highly sustainable.

Kinder Morgan’s valuation

The pipeline operator’s shares are valued at an enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratio of about 11.9X, which places KMI about mid-range in the industry group for large-cap North American midstream companies. Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Enbridge (ENB) and Williams Companies (WMB), three major rivals in the natural gas-focused midstream business, trade at EV-to-EBITDA ratios of 11.0X, 13.3X, and 14.4X, with the industry group average being 12.7X.

If Kinder Morgan revalues to the industry group average EBITDA multiplier, shares of the midstream platform continue to have upside potential in 2026, especially if the company can expand its pipeline footprint on growing natural gas demand. A 12.7X average EV-to-EBITDA ratio implies 7.1% revaluation potential as well as a fair value of $34 per-share for Kinder Morgan.

Primary risks for income investors

From a dividend coverage point of view, Kinder Morgan should have no problems supporting its dividend with distributable cash flow in 2026. The natural gas outlook is also solid, and so is the firm's natural gas segment performance. Nonetheless, there are risks that long-term investors have to factor into their analysis before buying shares of KMI. Specifically, there are three risks that could negatively impact my view on the pipeline operator going forward: 1) Slowing natural gas demand, potentially driven by a recession in the U.S., 2) Moderating growth in the buildout pace of U.S. Data Centers which is a key catalyst for natural gas-driven EBITDA and backlog growth; and 3) A drop in KMI's dividend coverage ratio below 2.0X.

Final thoughts

Natural gas activity continues to remain a significant earnings driver for Kinder Morgan in the first fiscal quarter. Most importantly, growing natural gas demand, in part driven by a growing U.S.-based Data Center footprint, translates into attractive earnings prospects for investors seeking recurring dividend income from an investment grade-rated midstream platform that is tilted towards natural gas deliveries. With Kinder Morgan transporting about 40% of all natural gas in the U.S., the pipeline operator remains a top bet on natural gas expansion and new pipeline acquisitions connecting the company’s facilities to vital production assets located in the Permian and other Basins. Since shares continue to trade below the industry group average, as highlighted above, a strong buy rating is still well-deserved, in my opinion.

data.title: The Trade Desk: Potential Value Trap, Pending FQ1 2026 And Competitive Clarity
data.description:
I previously downgraded The Trade Desk, Inc. (TTD) to a Hold in December 2025, attributed to the growth risks from the intensifying ad-tech competition.

In this article, I shall discuss why TTD remains a Hold, pending further clarity on their performance metrics in the upcoming FQ1'26 earnings call.

TTD Faces Numerous Headwinds Entering FY2026

For now, TTD has indeed lost -43.7% of its value while underperforming the wider market at +3.3% and many of its ad-tech peers in varying degrees.

The steep correction has lent credence to my prior downgraded rating, as TTD enters a period of decelerating growth profile, attributed to the intensifying ad-tech competition from numerous Big Tech companies.

1. FQ1'26 Preview

With TTD set to report their FQ1'26 earnings call on May 7, 2026, I urge readers to compare their performance metrics against the last offered guidance at:

  • the revenues of at least $678M (-19.9% QoQ/+10% YoY),
  • the adj. EBITDA of $195M (-51.2% QoQ/-6.2% YoY), and
  • the adj. EBITDA margins of 28.7% (-18.5 points QoQ/-5 YoY/-3.6 from FY2019 levels of 32.3%).

While the management expects to deliver "full year 2026 adjusted EBITDA margin percentage to be approximately in line with 2025" at 41.1% (-0.2 points YoY), I am of the opinion that their mixed FQ1'26 guidance has yet to inspire confidence.

This is especially since TTD has been reporting a decelerating revenue growth profile since 2022, with a similar trend also observed:

  • in FQ4'25 at +14% YoY against a year ago at +22% YoY, and
  • in FY2025 at +18% YoY against a year ago at +26% YoY.

These numbers naturally pale in comparison to its ad-tech peers' outperformance, including:

  • AppLovin (APP), with advertising revenue growth of +70% YoY in FY2025 against a year ago at +75% YoY,
  • Amazon (AMZN) at +22% YoY/+19.8% YoY,
  • Meta (META) at +22.1% YoY/+21.7% YoY, and
  • Google (GOOG) at +11.3% YoY/+11.2% YoY, respectively,

which perhaps explain why TTD's stock prices have underperformed its peers, despite the lifting market sentiments after the double bottoming observed in late March/early April 2026.

2. Changing Advertising Environment

Part of the headwinds may be attributed to two large advertising agencies, Dentsu and WPP plc, reportedly "pulling back from one of The Trade Desk’s most closely-watched, and arguably important, initiatives: OpenPath."

This is supposedly attributed to "concerns about economics and opacity. And because bigger DSP margins compound into higher customer-acquisition costs and weaker ROAS," where TTD supposedly "earns revenue on both sides of the transaction."

This perhaps explains TTD's rather elevated, estimated take rate of 21.5% in FY2025 (+0.8 points YoY), based on the revenues of $2.89B (+18.4% YoY) and the gross advertising spend of $13.39B (+11.2% YoY).

This is compared to AMZN's highly competitive rates at "between 4% and 8%. In some cases, those fees drop as low as 1%—and occasionally disappear altogether," with it underscoring the tech giant's willingness " to undercut the market to win share, especially when it comes to pulling dollars into its DSP."

At the same time, readers may want to note that OpenPath is supposedly TTD's answer to GOOG's Open Bidding, with the former "providing advertisers with direct access to premium digital advertising inventory that helps minimize waste for advertisers while maximizing revenue for participating publishers."

Therefore, while it is uncertain how accurate the market report may be on Dentsu/WPP's supposed withdrawal from TTD's OpenPath platform, it appears that the market has decided to de-risk first, as observed in TTD's underperforming stock prices.

Interested investors may want to tune in to the upcoming earnings call and hear out the management's commentary surrounding OpenPath's adoption trends.

3. CTV Prospects

TTD recently highlighted CTV being "a strong driver of overall growth and remains one of our fastest-growing channels," with it comprising approximately 50% of its business in FQ4'25 compared to "high-40s percentage share" in FQ4'24.

This development is partly attributed to their exposure to numerous publishers and the growing popularity of sports streaming.

TTD has also signaled their plans to expand beyond the US, since its current revenues are still concentrated domestically at 85% in FY2025 (-2 points YoY).

If anything, the international segment has already driven accelerated growth prospects by +34.7% YoY in FY2025 against a year ago at +24.9% YoY, against the decelerating growth observed in the US at +15.9% YoY in FY2025 against a year ago at +26% YoY.

Given the nascency of the international segment, I urge readers to monitor TTD's upcoming FQ1'26 execution and the management's FQ2'26 guidance, since it remains to be seen when their growth re-acceleration may occur.

My caution is attributed to the notably smaller "open web programmatic ad spending" prospects at $40.78B in FY2026 (+6.3% YoY), against walled gardens (such as those within AMZN, META, and GOOG) at $162.2B (+14.2% YoY), with it presenting TTD's growth headwinds against the more aggressive Big Tech peer.

4. Growth Headwinds Already Priced In

Given the decelerating growth trends and the mixed FQ1'26 guidance, it is unsurprising that the consensus has already downgraded their forward estimates, with TTD expected to report a decelerating top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +11%/+14.5% through FY2028.

This is compared to its historical growth profile at a CAGR of +27.9%/+29.9% between FY2019 and FY2025, respectively.

Combined with the -75.2% correction from the 52-week high, TTD has been discounted to FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 7.60x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 11.26x.

This is down from the recent highs of 37.10x/49.59x and the 5Y mean of 45.14x/61.11x, albeit nearer to the sector median of 7.49x/13.93x, respectively.

When comparing TTD's DSP prospects at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 7.60x and adj. EBITDA margins of 41.1% in FY2025 against its fellow DSP/SSP peers, including:

  • DSP/SSP and hyperscaler/commerce - AMZN at 13.46x/20.3% in FY2025,
  • SSP - Magnite (MGNI) at 7.20x/32.5% in FY2025,
  • DSP/SSP and hyperscaler - GOOG at 18.39x/37.2% in FY2025,
  • DSP/SSP and social media - META at 11.87x/50.6% in FY2025, and
  • DSP/SSP - APP at 22.79x/82.2% in FY2025, respectively,

it may first appear that TTD remains attractively valued here, assuming that they are able to deliver their preliminary FY2026 profit margin guidance.

This development implies that the ongoing 2025/Q1'26 meltdown may have been overdone, albeit with it triggering TTD's potential, deep value play opportunity for the contrarian.

TTD Remains A Hold, Pending Further Clarity

For now, given TTD's ongoing meltdown and the breach against numerous support levels, it is uncertain when a trading floor may materialize.

TTD has also been oversold for some time, signaling a lack of bullish support, worsened by the potential volatility from the elevated short interest ratio of +15.6% at the time of writing.

Based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 11.26x (near the all-time P/E lows of 9.75x) and the FY2025 adj EPS performance of $1.77 (+6.6% YoY), the stock appears to be trading near to my base-case fair value estimates of $19.90.

Based on the consensus FY2028 adj. EPS estimates of $2.65, there appears to be an excellent upside potential of +27.7% to my base-case, long-term price target of $29.80 as well.

On the other hand, pending further clarity on the FQ1'26 performance and the FQ2'26 guidance, I am of the opinion that TTD may present a potential value trap thesis here, as similarly observed in the management's underwhelming FQ1'26 guidance.

This is worsened by the uncertainty from the ongoing 'executive departures' and 'audit issues,' with it remaining to be seen how things may develop in the near-term.

Combined with the decelerating growth profile, the lumpy profit margin performance, and the oversold technical indicators/discounted valuations implying its value trap investment thesis, I believe that it may be more prudent to wait on the sidelines and observe their near-term performance instead.

TTD remains a Hold here.

data.title: Alibaba's AI Engine Is Powering A Still Undervalued Comeback
data.description:
In early December 2025, I wrote my last article about Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA) and as the stock has already declined 20% from its previous high, I argued that investors could take advantage of the recent setback. In the conclusion of my article I wrote:

In my opinion, Alibaba remains one of the better investments for the long run. The company is investing heavily in AI and it is a very diversified business that might profit in several ways from the AI-revolution. And while Alibaba might be able to grow at a high pace (high single digits or even double digits) for several years, the stock is still trading for a more reasonable valuation multiple than several U.S. AI companies.

Of course, this is not the best argument to make, but we should not ignore that Alibaba is still trading about 50% below its all-time high – indicating at least that sentiment was once a lot more bullish. And I see the previous all-time high certainly as a reasonable target within the next one to three years as Alibaba should be able to grow the stock price at a high pace and certainly outperform the market.

However, the stock continued to decline further and dropped below $120 in early April, which resulted in a 37% decline from its previous high. In the last few days, the stock jumped – in line with many other stocks around the world, raising the question whether Alibaba found its bottom.

In the following article, I will argue once again that Alibaba is continuing to trade below its intrinsic value and remains a good long-term investment in my opinion. We will look at the company’s core business (the consumption business), which will also contribute to growth, but not as much as the cloud and AI business, which is currently responsible for a huge part of growth. Finally, we are looking at T-Head, the semiconductor business of Alibaba.

Quarterly Results

We start by looking at third quarter results, which were reported on March 19, 2026 and were rather disappointing. Revenue increased from RMB 280,154 million in Q3/25 to RMB 284,843 million in Q3/26 – resulting in 1.7% year-over-year growth. While the top line still increased a little bit, income from operations dropped steeply from RMB 41,205 million in the same quarter last year to RMB 10,645 million in this quarter – resulting in a 74.2% year-over-year decline. Finally, diluted earnings per share declined 71.0% year-over-year from RMB 2.55 in the same quarter last year to RMB 0.74 in this quarter.

However, we must look at the numbers in more detail as the picture is not as bad as it looks at first glance. Similar to many other AI companies, Alibaba increased its expenditures in the last few years and especially sales and marketing expenses jumped from RMB 42.7 billion in the same quarter last year to RMB 72.0 billion in this quarter – primarily due to the investments in user experiences of the Alibaba China E-commerce Group. We must also point out that Alibaba sold Intime in December 2024 and Sun Art in January 2025, which still had a negative impact on the income statement in the third quarter. When excluding these effects, total revenue increased 9% year-over-year.

Growth Potential

When talking about Alibaba’s growth potential in the years to come, we can focus on three pillars of growth and we start with the core business of Alibaba, which is sometimes overlooked these days as everybody is focusing on cloud and AI. The core business of Alibaba is still its consumption business – mostly the e-commerce business in China, but also its international business.

Consumption Businesses

Alibaba China E-commerce Group, the core business of Alibaba, increased its revenue from RMB 150,589 million in Q3/25 to RMB 159,347 million in Q3/26 – resulting in 5.8% year-over-year growth. But while revenue still increased in the mid-single digits, adjusted EBITDA declined 43% year-over-year to RMB 34,613 million.

And this segment is still struggling a little bit as the China retail sales are still growing, but at a rather slow pace. In March 2026, Chinese retail sales increased 1.7%, which is still a solid growth, but compared to previous growth rates in China, this is rather disappointing.

Especially the quick commerce business is growing with a high pace and increased 56% year-over-year to RMB 20,828 million in this quarter. The Taobao app also saw its monthly active customers increase in the double-digits year-over-year and the 88VIP members, who spend the most on the platform, is still growing in the double-digits and surpassed 59 million.

On the other hand, revenue from customer management, which is responsible for the biggest part of sales, is struggling a little bit. Revenue still increased 0.8% year-over-year to RMB 102,664 million due to weaker transaction activity and the phase-out of the impact of software service fee implementation.

When talking about the retail business (or consumption business), we should not only look at China, but also at the Alibaba International Digital Commerce Group, the international business. In the last quarter it also increased its revenue 3.8% year-over-year to RMB 39,201 million. Adjusted EBITDA for the segment was negative once again, but the picture improved. Instead of a negative amount of RMB 4,952 million in the same quarter last year it was only RMB 2,016 million in this quarter.

Cloud and AI

Growth, however, was mostly driven by the Cloud Intelligence Group, which increased revenue from RMB 31,742 million in Q3/25 to RMB 43,284 million in Q3/26 – resulting in 36.4% year-over-year growth. Adjusted EBITDA also increased 24.6% year-over-year from RMB 3,138 million to RMB 3,911 million.

Management is expecting that growth will stem from three different sources in the years to come. The first will be the MaaS business, Model-as-a-service. Alibaba is expecting that this business will be supported by a variety of different use cases (including the company’s own applications as well as a diverse array of AI application scenarios). Alibaba could generate revenue on a per token basis.

A second driver of growth might be the demand for enterprise-level internal inference and training, which would be like a new marketplace. And management is optimistic this will be an important and long-lasting market:

And that market will continue to exist in the long term. It's not one that will disappear simply because each enterprise makes decisions based on its own business model and the security requirements of its specific use case or the particularity of an application scenario. So for some application scenarios, enterprises will opt to use public MaaS API services, while many others will be based on privately deployed solutions within the enterprise.

Finally, Alibaba is also expecting the “traditional” cloud business to continue growing in the years to come. The CPU-centric cloud computing still has significant room for expansion in this AI-enabled era. The shift towards AI agents might also generate growing demand. During the last earnings call, management commented:

However, in the future, there could be billions of agents that are created by large AI models and their operating environment. The operating environment of these agents will also require substantial support from traditional CPU-centric cloud computing. They need these traditional CPUs as well as databases, storage and large amounts of memory to support their long-term problem solving and sustained operations.

And management is especially optimistic for its combined cloud and AI business and made the following statement during the last earnings call:

Given the enormous and sustained growth momentum of the AI market, combined with Alibaba's full stack positioning across the AI value chain, the business goal of Alibaba's AI strategy is very clear. Over the next 5 years, our goal is to surpass USD 100 billion in combined cloud and AI external revenue, including MaaS.

Looking at the results reported in the last few quarters, Alibaba can certainly be optimistic as revenue for AI-related products was growing in the triple-digits year-over-year for the tenth consecutive quarters.

T-Head

There is a third pillar that might contribute to growth – Alibaba’s semiconductor business T-Head, which was founded in 2018 as a wholly owned subsidiary. T-Head has now shipped about 470,000 AI chips in total so far and the company is reaching an annual revenue level around RMB 10 billion.

Alibaba is not really selling its chips, but it is rather using them internally for its own computing power. But these chips are contributing to the cloud and AI results. During the last earnings call, management was pointing out that over 60% of T-Head chips are being used by external commercial customers – including companies from multiple industries like internet finance, autonomous driving and intelligent manufacturing.

And while acknowledging that T-Head is lagging Western chip makers like NVIDIA, management is seeing the business playing an important role in the next few years and underlined how it is a huge part in Alibaba’s strategy – especially for supply chain resilience. During the last earnings call, management commented:

Because I believe that over the next 3 to 5 years, global AI computing power will be an extremely short supply, especially in the Chinese market, as the only cloud compute company in the Chinese market with proprietary chip development capabilities. T-Head is of paramount importance, therefore, to the Alibaba Group, increasing the supply of AI computing power will help our cloud and AI businesses, including our MaaS business to achieve stronger growth momentum.

It was also reported in January 2026 that Alibaba is planning an IPO for its T-Head subsidiary, but it is unclear when this might happen. In a first step the unit might be restructured as a business partly owned by employees. When asked by analysts during the earnings call, management was not really commenting on more detailed plans about the IPO. It just stated that an IPO is not out of the picture, but a clear timeline does not exist.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

In a final step, we must determine an intrinsic value for Alibaba once again and in a first step we could look at the two simple valuation multiples – the price-earnings ratio as well as the price-free-cash-flow ratio. Both metrics were kind of volatile in the last few months and we should therefore question whether we can draw conclusions from the simple valuation multiples.

At the time of writing, Alibaba was trading for 26 times free cash flow and earnings per share. This is not really indicating that Alibaba is a bargain at this point, but instead of using the simple valuation multiples, I will rather use a discount cash flow calculation to determine an intrinsic value.

As always, I am calculating with a discount rate of 10% as this is the annual return I like to achieve by an investment annually. Additionally, we are calculating with the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (19,310 million) and as we are calculating with the ADR, we use 2,414 million (exchange rate of 8:1).

Now we could use the free cash flow of the last four quarters as a basis, which was $12,980 million. But we should point out that Alibaba was investing heavily in the last few years (similar to many other technology companies) and the free cash flow of the last four quarters might not necessarily be representative. In the last four quarters, Alibaba reported a free cash flow margin of only 9.2% - clearly below the long-term average of 34.4% (since the IPO). And while we can make the argument that margins were constantly declining in the last few years, which is not a good sign, the current free cash flow margin seems unreasonably low. And similar to many other technology companies, Alibaba has extremely high capital expenditures right now.

Instead, we should be a little more optimistic and assume at least $20 billion in free cash flow as basis here. Additionally, we are calculating with 5% growth for the next ten years (a rather cautious assumption considering the Chinese GDP growth is also expected in the low-to-mid single digits) followed by 4% growth till perpetuity. This leads to an intrinsic value of $147.53 for Alibaba and the stock is more or less fairly valued.

When looking at analysts’ estimates for the years to come, we should calculate with much higher rates. Analysts assume growth rates at least in the high single digits (or maybe even double digits). Let’s calculate with 8% growth in a second calculation followed by 4% growth till perpetuity. This leads to an intrinsic value of $180.12 for Alibaba and would make the stock undervalued at this point.

Finally, I like to raise two more points here. First, a free cash flow of $20 billion still seems like a moderate assumption as Alibaba should have the potential to report a higher free cash flow margin in the years to come.

Second, we could also make the argument for a higher growth rate in 10 years from now. Not only is Alibaba a high-quality business, but the Chinese economy will also grow with a higher pace. When the economy is already growing 4% annually, we can assume that Alibaba might be able to grow 5% or 6% over the long run. This is also making an intrinsic value of $200 to $250 possible.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Alibaba remains undervalued and also is a good long-term investment at this point. The setback in the last few months generated a good buying opportunity.

Despite the challenges, Alibaba is facing – including still rather low retail sales and China probably being affected by the oil shortage and the blocked Strait of Hormuz, which might put the economy under pressure – I remain optimistic about the long-term potential of the business. Especially the company’s cloud and AI business might lead to higher growth rates over several years.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Equity LifeStyle Properties: A Structural Compounder Trading At A Discount
data.description:
Introduction

Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS) owns and operates a portfolio of manufactured home (“MH”) communities, recreational vehicle (“RV”) resorts, campgrounds and marinas in North America. While I have written about other residential REITs previously, most recently on UMH Properties (UMH) last month, this will be my first time covering Equity LifeStyle Properties. The company recently released its Q1 2026 earnings, so I figure now would be a good time to evaluate the company.

Business Model

As of 31 March 2026, Equity LifeStyle Properties owns or holds an interest in 453 properties in across 35 states and British Columbia, comprising around 173,400 total sites. Its portfolio spans MH communities (75,700 sites), RV resorts (34,600 annual, 9,800 seasonal, 20,500 transient sites), marina slips (6,900 sites) and membership campground sites (26,000 sites).

The company’s business model is built around land ownership. The company owns the land and leases the developed sites to customers either on a long-term or short-term basis, which creates predictable annual revenue streams. Approximately 91% of revenue comes from stable, annual sources, insulating the company from economic volatility.

Q1 2026 Performance

Equity LifeStyle Properties reported Normalized Funds From Operations (FFO) of $0.84/share, in line with the midpoint of prior guidance of $0.81/share to $0.87/share. Total revenues rose by 2.7% compared to the same period last year, from $387.3 million to $397.6 million. The MH segment, which is also the company’s biggest segment, was the standout performer with base rental income increasing by 5.7% to $195.1 million. This increase was largely drive by an increase in the monthly base average rent per site from $895 to $948, a 5.9% increase. Overall, the company saw a 3.7% increase in property operating revenues.

On the cost side, core property operating expenses rose by just 1.8%, reflecting a positive spread to revenue growth. Additionally, the company completed its property and casualty insurance renewal in April with a premium decrease of approximately 18% while maintaining the same coverage. The savings from this should contribute positively to 2026 margins.

However, there was some weakness as well. Core RV and marina base rental income saw a 1.4% decrease, largely driven by sharp drops in seasonal (-14.8%) and transient (-6.9%) revenue. As a result, management revised its full year 2026 guidance for this segment downwards, though it maintained full year 2026 guidance for Normalized FFO at between $3.12/share and $3.22/share.

Growing Demand… And Limited Supply

Perhaps the most significant advantage Equity LifeStyle Properties has is that it caters to a growing demographic, while at the same time having limited competition.

Over 70% of the company’s MH properties are either age-qualified or have a resident base with an average over 55. Moreover, over 50% of the company’s MH residents are aged 70 or older. Each day, around 10,000 Americans turn 65, and the projected population of people aged 55+ is expected to grow by 14% from 2025 to 2040, ensuring that demand for the company’s properties remain strong and durable. Moreover, the value proposition of purchasing a MH is clear, with a new MH costing 75% less compared to a new single-family home ($125,117 vs $505,867).

It is on the supply front where the company has its biggest competitive advantage. There has been a limited number of new MH developments over the past 20 years, driven by restrictive zoning and regulations. This reduces competition for the company, making replicating its portfolio almost impossible.

With a limited supply and an ever-growing demand, it is no surprise that the company’s occupancy has consistently remained high despite increasing rental prices. While the monthly base average rent per site for Q1 2026 saw a 5.9% increase compared to the same period last year, the company’s occupancy rate remained high at 93.8%, with 50% of communities at 98%+ occupancy.

A Quality Balance Sheet

Equity LifeStyle Properties has a strong balance sheet with low leverage. As of 31 March 2026, the company’s total debt stands at $3.3 billion, giving it a debt to EBITDA ratio of 4.5x. Its maturity schedule is well-laddered, with an average maturity of 8 years, and a weighted average interest rate of 4.1%. Just 6% of its debt is due by 2027, giving the company a long runway and eliminating any near-term refinancing risk. Additionally, 97% of its debt is fixed rate, insulating the company from interest rate volatility and ensuring predictability in cash flows.

In terms of liquidity, the company has around $1.2 billion of capital available to its from its combined line of credit and ATM programs. This is more than sufficient for the company to fund its expansions and acquisitions to continue growing the company.

Reliable and Growing Dividend

When it comes to dividends, the company has an exceptional track record. The company currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.5425/share. Annualized, this equates to $2.17/share and a forward dividend yield of approximately 3.43%. This represents a 5.3% increase over the 2025 dividend, extending the company’s streak to 22 consecutive years of dividend growth. Its 10-year dividend CAGR of 10.6% is also more than double the 4.3% average in the REIT industry.

As mentioned above, the company currently projects Normalized FFO to come between $3.12/share and $3.22/share. Even at the lower end of guidance, this gives the company a dividend payout ratio of 69.55%, ensuring that its dividend sustainability is not in question and also opening the door to further increases.

Key Risks

The clearest near-term risk to the company lies in its RV portfolio. For Q1 2026, the RV portfolio saw a decline in seasonal and transient revenue, leading the company to lower its full-year guidance for this segment. If this weakness persists, it would impact the company’s earnings. However, there are a couple of mitigating factors. First, the RV portfolio represents a smaller portion of the company’s earnings (22.5%) and its impact will not be overly detrimental if the MH segment continues to outperform. Additionally, the annual revenue for the segment actually increased during the quarter (4.2%), which leads me to believe that the current weakness is more seasonal instead of structural.

Another risk is that of interest rates. While the company has done well to lock in the bulk of its debt at a fixed interest rate, a rise in interest rates will still negatively impact the company, especially if it needs to touch on its floating-rate line of credit facility.

Trading At A Premium or Fairly Valued?

Equity LifeStyle Properties is currently trading at approximately 20x forward Normalized FFO. This is significantly higher than the sector median, and at first glance it would seem as if the company is overvalued.

However, the company is trading at a premium for several reasons. The company has consistently outperformed its peers, with its 8.2% Normalized FFO CAGR from 2006 to 2025 almost double that of the 4.2% average for its peers in the residential REIT industry. The company has also shown remarkable stability through various economic cycles. Since 2001, the company has delivered positive NOI growth in virtually every year.

This sentiment is clearly shared by analysts. The average analyst price target for the company is $70.47/share, representing an 11% upside. The consensus rating for the company is also a “Buy”. If anything, it seems to me that analysts view the company as undervalued.

Conclusion

Equity LifeStyle Properties is a high-quality, recession-resistant business which has consistently outperformed its peers. While its dividend is relatively low at 3.43%, the company has been growing its dividends for 22 years, and its comfortable dividend payout ratio indicates this streak will continue for some time to come. At first glance, the company may seem to be overvalued, but there are multiple reasons why it is trading at a premium compared to its peers. For all these reasons, I rate the company as a “Buy” with a price target of $70/share.
